ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers tea infusions broadly; no Pu'er-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Pu'er tea as a food substance; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed Pu'er tea is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing roughly 2 kcal and negligible macronutrients.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171917
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Pu'er tea is a post-fermented variety of Camellia sinensis. No Pu'er-specific entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. USDA SR Legacy 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 171917) is used as the closest approved-source equivalent for the brewed liquid. Mineral values (potassium 18 mg, calcium 2 mg, magnesium 2 mg, iron 0.04 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA data for brewed tea. Adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of foods: a physiological basis for carbohydrate exchange — Jenkins et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Brewed Pu'er tea contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g (0.5 g per 250 ml cup). Glycemic index is not applicable for this beverage as it contains negligible carbohydrates and has no meaningful glycemic effect. Per established peer-reviewed GI methodology (Jenkins et al., Am J Clin Nutr 1981), foods with negligible available carbohydrate cannot elicit a measurable glycemic response and are classified as GI not applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation. glycemic_index field is set to null per Veritas rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (0.8 mg/100 g); caffeine may transiently raise blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed Pu'er tea is an appropriate beverage for individuals with hypertension given its negligible sodium content; however, those who are caffeine-sensitive should limit intake, particularly in the evening, as caffeine can transiently elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.2 g/100 g); polyphenols may support insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened Pu'er tea is an excellent beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains negligible carbohydrates and does not raise blood glucose; emerging evidence suggests its polyphenols may modestly improve insulin sensitivity, but no sugar or milk should be added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, rich in polyphenols and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pu'er tea contains no fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, making it a heart-friendly beverage; its polyphenol content may support vascular endothelial function, though individuals on anticoagulant therapy should note that tea contains small amounts of vitamin K.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols; caffeine may affect calcium absorption
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pu'er tea's antioxidant and polyphenol content may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; excessive consumption should be avoided as high caffeine intake may interfere with calcium absorption and sleep quality, both important for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pu'er tea (普洱茶, Pǔ'ěr chá) is a unique variety of post-fermented tea produced exclusively in Yunnan province, China, from the large-leaf Camellia sinensis var. assamica cultivar. Unlike green or black teas, Pu'er undergoes a secondary microbial fermentation and oxidation process after the leaves have been dried and rolled, and may be aged for several years or even decades. It is commercially available in two main categories: Sheng (raw/green) Pu'er, which is naturally aged and develops complexity over time, and Shou (ripe/cooked) Pu'er, which undergoes an accelerated wet-piling fermentation process. The brewed liquor is characteristically deep reddish-brown to dark amber in colour, with an earthy, woody, and sometimes leather-like flavour profile, occasionally with notes of dried fruit or subtle sweetness in aged varieties. In Chinese culinary culture, Pu'er is the quintessential tea served during dim sum (yum cha) because it is traditionally believed to cut through grease and aid digestion after rich, oily meals. Nutritionally, brewed Pu'er tea is essentially a near-zero-calorie beverage: per 100 ml it provides approximately 0.8 kcal, 0.04 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 0.2 g carbohydrate, with trace minerals including potassium (18 mg), calcium (2 mg), and magnesium (2 mg). It contains no sugar, no fat, and negligible sodium. Its glycemic index is not applicable given its negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed unsweetened. It contains caffeine, which should be considered by caffeine-sensitive individuals. The tea can be steeped multiple times, with flavour evolving across infusions.
common_user_queries: Is Chinese tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in a cup of Pu'er tea?
Is Pu-erh tea suitable for hypertension?
What tea is served at dim sum?
What is the difference between Sheng and Shou Pu'er?
Is fermented tea good for digestion?

confidence_reason: No Pu'er-specific entry exists in the Singapore HPB database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 171917), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for brewed Camellia sinensis tea. Values are consistent with the input record and plausible for a brewed tea beverage. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: fat_g is naturally zero — brewed tea contains no fat by nature of the brewing process (confirmed per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917). sugar_g is naturally zero — no sugars are present in plain unsweetened brewed tea (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917). dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — the leaf solids are not consumed in brewed tea. 2. SOURCE ADAPTATION: No Pu'er-specific entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of April 2026. USDA SR Legacy 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 171917) is used as the closest approved-source equivalent. Mineral values in the input record (potassium 18 mg, calcium 2 mg, magnesium 2 mg, iron 0.04 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA data. 3. GI NOT APPLICABLE: Brewed Pu'er tea contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making glycemic index measurement not applicable. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per Veritas rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. GI source references Jenkins et al. (Am J Clin Nutr, 1981) as the peer-reviewed methodological basis for the not-applicable classification. 4. PORTIONS: Input contained duplicate portion entries. Deduplicated to two canonical portions: 1 cup (250 g) and 1 serving (200 ml / 200 g). 5. CAFFEINE: Pu'er tea contains caffeine (approximately 30–70 mg per 250 ml cup depending on steeping time and grade), which is clinically relevant for hypertension and RA management. Caffeine content is not captured in per_100g as it is not a standard nutrient field in the template. 6. VITAMIN K: A small amount of vitamin K (0.1 mcg/100 g) is present; clinically relevant for patients on warfarin or other vitamin K-antagonist anticoagulants. 7. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no Pu'er-specific codes exist in public ontology catalogues as of April 2026.
